channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Poland wants to ban vapes and nicotine pouches must taste like tobacco ”only favours the cigarette trade”
item.description: Poland’s proposed restrictions on vaping products and nicotine pouches are drawing criticism from harm reduction advocates and public health commentators across Europe. Concerns focus particularly on plans that could limit flavours to tobacco-only varieties, with critics arguing that such measures may reduce the appeal of lower-risk alternatives for adult smokers attempting to switch away from cigarettes. Researchers and policy analysts continue to debate whether increasingly restrictive nicotine regulation adequately reflects differences in relative risk between combustible and non-combustible products. The controversy highlights broader tensions between precautionary regulation, smoking cessation objectives, and evidence-based tobacco harm reduction policy. (Published on May 21, 2026byvejpkollen.se)

item.title: Vape Taxes Won’t Increase Smoking, Study Claims. Here’s the Problem.
item.description: New research examining the relationship between vape taxation and smoking behaviour is intensifying debate over the unintended consequences of nicotine regulation. Researchers warn that higher taxes on vaping products may reduce switching away from combustible cigarettes by making lower-risk alternatives less financially accessible to adult smokers. The findings contribute to growing evidence that regulatory measures affecting non-combustible nicotine products can influence smoking prevalence, consumer behaviour, and public health outcomes in complex ways. The debate reflects broader tensions between revenue generation, youth protection policies, and harm reduction strategies aimed at reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 22, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: EU seeks public input on tobacco regulation revisions
item.description: The European Union’s ongoing review of tobacco and nicotine legislation is reopening debate over the future direction of tobacco harm reduction policy across Europe. Public consultations are expected to address issues including vaping products, nicotine pouches, taxation, flavour restrictions, and cross-border regulation of non-combustible nicotine alternatives. Researchers, public health experts, industry groups, and consumer advocates continue to disagree over how regulation should balance youth protection with the potential benefits of reduced-risk products for adult smokers. The consultation process may play a significant role in shaping future European policy on smoking cessation, nicotine risk communication, and harm reduction strategies. (Published on May 22, 2026bysnusforumet.se)

item.title: New Data Contradicts Scaremongering
item.description: New data on smoking and nicotine use is challenging increasingly common claims surrounding the risks and population impact of vaping products. Researchers and harm reduction advocates argue that recent findings do not support narratives suggesting that vaping is driving increases in cigarette smoking or undermining public health progress. Instead, some datasets continue to show declining smoking prevalence occurring alongside greater uptake of non-combustible nicotine alternatives among adult smokers. The debate highlights ongoing tensions between precautionary public health messaging, interpretation of emerging evidence, and the role of harm reduction strategies in reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 22, 2026byplanetofthevapes.co.uk)

item.title: Cannabis Enforcement Changes Coming To Australian State
item.description: Proposed changes to cannabis enforcement policy in an Australian state are reigniting debate over the effectiveness of prohibition-based drug regulation. Supporters of reform argue that criminal penalties for personal cannabis use may have limited public health benefit while contributing to unnecessary legal and social consequences. The discussion also reflects broader international trends toward harm reduction, decriminalization, and regulatory approaches focused more on public health outcomes than punitive enforcement. As governments continue reassessing nicotine, tobacco, and cannabis policy, questions are increasingly being raised about how regulation can balance risk reduction, consumer behaviour, and evidence-based public health objectives. (Published on May 22, 2026byinternationalcbc.com)
